annotations.label: Metric tensor (informal)

anchor.snippet: a metric tensor (or simply metric ) is an additional structure on a manifold

annotations.note: Mathlib's `RiemannianMetric` packages a smoothly/continuously varying inner product on the tangent (or any) bundle, matching the informal notion.

annotations.label: Positive-definite metric tensor and Riemannian manifold

anchor.snippet: A manifold equipped with a positive-definite metric tensor is known as a Riemannian manifold

annotations.note: `IsRiemannianManifold I M` is the Prop-valued typeclass for a manifold with a (positive-definite) Riemannian metric on its tangent spaces.

annotations.label: Metric tensor as covariant symmetric tensor

anchor.snippet: Thus a metric tensor is a covariant symmetric tensor

annotations.note: The structure `RiemannianMetric` stores `inner b : E b →L[ℝ] E b →L[ℝ] ℝ` with a `symm` field, i.e., a symmetric covariant 2-tensor on the bundle.

annotations.label: Parametric surface

anchor.snippet: Thus a parametric surface is (in today's terms) a vector-valued function

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `ParametricSurface` concept; one would work directly with smooth maps `ℝ² → ℝ³`.

annotations.label: Arc length on a parametric surface

anchor.snippet: The arc length of that curve is given by the integral

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `pathELength` for paths in Riemannian manifolds and `eVariationOn` for arc length in metric spaces, but no surface-specific arc-length integral via a first fundamental form.

annotations.label: Line element and first fundamental form

anchor.snippet: The quantity ds in ( 1 ) is called the line element , while ds 2 is called the first fundamental form

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `lineElement` or `firstFundamentalForm` definition for surfaces in ℝ³.

annotations.label: Metric tensor of a surface (via transformation law)

anchor.snippet: with the transformation law ( 3 ) is known as the metric tensor of the surface

annotations.note: Coordinate-based, transformation-law-driven surface metric is not present, but `RiemannianMetric` is the coordinate-free generalization.

annotations.label: Invariance of first fundamental form under coordinate change

anchor.section: Invariance of arclength under coordinate transformations
anchor.snippet: the first fundamental form ( 1 ) is invariant under changes in the coordinate system

annotations.note: No coordinate-transformation theorem for the first fundamental form is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inner product of tangent vectors via metric

anchor.snippet: the metric tensor allows one to compute the dot product of tangent vectors in a manner independent of the parametric description

annotations.note: Once a continuous Riemannian bundle structure is fixed, the inner product `⟪v, w⟫` on tangent fibers is well-defined and behaves continuously.

annotations.label: Symmetry and bilinearity of the surface metric

anchor.snippet: This is a symmetric function in a and b

annotations.note: `RiemannianMetric.symm` enforces symmetry, and bilinearity is built into the `→L[ℝ] _ →L[ℝ] ℝ` shape of `inner`.

annotations.label: Length of a tangent vector

anchor.snippet: the length of a tangent vector a is given by

annotations.note: The induced norm on tangent spaces is `‖v‖ = √⟪v, v⟫`, coming from the `InnerProductSpace ℝ (TangentSpace I x)` instance derived from `RiemannianBundle`.

annotations.label: Angle between tangent vectors

anchor.snippet: the angle θ between two vectors a and b is calculated by

annotations.note: `InnerProductGeometry.angle` defines the angle in an inner product space, which applies fiberwise to tangent spaces but is not specifically packaged for Riemannian manifolds.

annotations.label: Surface area via parameterization

anchor.snippet: the surface area of M is given by the integral

annotations.note: Mathlib has no surface-area formula for parametrized surfaces (no `surfaceArea` over `‖∂u × ∂v‖`).

annotations.label: Surface area via determinant (Lagrange's identity)

anchor.snippet: By Lagrange's identity for the cross product, the integral can be written

annotations.note: No surface-area-as-determinant formulation is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metric tensor at a point

anchor.snippet: A metric tensor at p is a function g p ( X p , Y p ) which takes as inputs a pair of tangent vectors

annotations.note: `RiemannianMetric.inner b : E b →L[ℝ] E b →L[ℝ] ℝ` is exactly the pointwise metric tensor at `b`.

annotations.label: Metric tensor field on a manifold

anchor.snippet: A metric tensor field g on M assigns to each point p of M a metric tensor

annotations.note: `ContMDiffRiemannianMetric` assigns a smooth family of inner products to each tangent fiber; with `IsContMDiffRiemannianBundle` recording smoothness.

anchors.snippet: A metric tensor field g on M assigns to each point p of M a metric tensor

annotations.label: Components of the metric in a frame

anchor.snippet: The components of the metric in any basis of vector fields , or frame

annotations.note: Mathlib's `RiemannianMetric` is intrinsic and does not unpack `g_{ij}` components in a chosen frame.

annotations.label: Covariant transformation of metric components

anchor.snippet: the system of quantities g ij [ f ] is said to transform covariantly with respect to changes in the frame

annotations.note: There is no `g_{ij}` change-of-frame transformation law in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metric components in coordinates

anchor.snippet: The metric g has components relative to this frame given by

annotations.note: Mathlib does not expose coordinate components `g(∂ᵢ, ∂ⱼ)` of a Riemannian metric in a chart.

annotations.label: Transformation of metric components under coordinate change

anchor.snippet: This new system of functions is related to the original g ij ( f ) by means of the chain rule

annotations.note: No statement of the chain-rule transformation law for metric components in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positive-definite metric and Riemannian metric

anchor.snippet: If the metric is positive-definite at every m ∈ M , then g is called a Riemannian metric

annotations.note: The `pos` field of `RiemannianMetric` enforces positive-definiteness fiberwise, matching the Riemannian-metric definition.

annotations.label: Pseudo-Riemannian metric and signature

anchor.snippet: the signature of g is this signature, and g is called a pseudo-Riemannian metric

annotations.note: Mathlib has no pseudo-Riemannian metric (its Riemannian bundle is by definition positive-definite).

annotations.label: Sylvester's law of inertia for metric diagonalization

anchor.snippet: By Sylvester's law of inertia , a basis of tangent vectors X i can be chosen locally

annotations.note: Sylvester's law of inertia is proved for quadratic forms on vector spaces, but not transported to the local diagonalization of a (pseudo-)Riemannian metric on a manifold.

annotations.label: Signature is constant on connected manifold

anchor.snippet: If M is connected , then the signature of q m does not depend on m

annotations.note: No statement about constancy of the signature of a (pseudo-)metric on a connected manifold.

annotations.label: Signature of a metric

anchor.snippet: The signature of g is the pair of integers ( p , n − p )

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm.sigPos`/`sigNeg` give the signature for a quadratic form on a vector space, but no `signature` of a metric tensor on a manifold.

annotations.label: Lorentzian and ultrahyperbolic metrics

anchor.snippet: If M is four-dimensional with signature (1, 3) or (3, 1) , then the metric is called Lorentzian

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define Lorentzian or ultrahyperbolic metrics.

annotations.label: Inverse metric

anchor.snippet: One can consider the inverse matrix G [ f ] −1 , which is identified with the inverse metric

annotations.note: There is no `inverseMetric` in Mathlib for a Riemannian bundle.

annotations.label: Contravariant transformation of inverse metric

anchor.snippet: The inverse metric transforms contravariantly

annotations.note: Contravariant transformation law for the inverse metric is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Basis-independence of inverse metric on covectors

anchor.snippet: The resulting definition, although it involves the choice of basis f , does not actually depend on f in an essential way

annotations.note: No statement of basis-independence for an inverse metric on cotangent vectors.

annotations.label: Lowering the index

anchor.snippet: is called lowering the index

annotations.note: Index-lowering on a single inner product space is realized by `toDualMap : E →ₗᵢ⋆[𝕜] StrongDual 𝕜 E`, but no fiberwise/manifold-level index-lowering operator is provided.

annotations.label: Raising the index

anchor.snippet: associating to the (covariant) components of a covector a [ f ] the (contravariant) components of a vector v [ f ] given is called raising the index

annotations.note: The inverse `(InnerProductSpace.toDual ℝ E).symm` realizes index-raising on inner product spaces; no Riemannian-bundle version exists.

annotations.label: Immersion and immersed submanifold

anchor.snippet: The mapping φ is called an immersion if its differential is injective at every point

annotations.note: `IsImmersion I J n f` captures `f` having injective differential at every point of a smooth manifold.

annotations.label: First fundamental form (induced metric in ℝ³)

anchor.snippet: the induced metric tensor is called the first fundamental form

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `firstFundamentalForm` for an immersed surface in ℝ³.

annotations.label: Pushforward of a tangent vector

anchor.snippet: This is called the pushforward of v along φ

annotations.note: `mfderiv I J f x : TangentSpace I x →L[ℝ] TangentSpace J (f x)` is the pushforward / differential of `f` at `x`.

annotations.label: Induced metric

anchor.snippet: the induced metric is defined by

annotations.note: Pullback / induced Riemannian metric along an immersion is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix form of induced metric via Jacobian

anchor.snippet: It follows from a straightforward calculation that the matrix of the induced metric

annotations.note: No `Jᵀ G J` matrix formula for the induced metric is present.

annotations.label: Intrinsic metric tensor via fiber bundles

anchor.snippet: a metric tensor is a function

annotations.note: `RiemannianMetric` (and `ContinuousRiemannianMetric`) gives the intrinsic fiber-bundle definition of a metric.

annotations.label: Metric as section of dual tensor product bundle

anchor.section: Metric as a section of a bundle
anchor.snippet: any bilinear mapping ( 10 ) gives rise naturally to a section g ⊗

annotations.note: `IsContinuousRiemannianBundle` records the continuous section `b ↦ g b : E b →L[ℝ] E b →L[ℝ] ℝ`, i.e., a continuous section of the bilinear-form bundle, without invoking a `T*M ⊗ T*M` construction.

annotations.label: Symmetry of g⊗ as tensor

anchor.section: Metric as a section of a bundle
anchor.snippet: Since g is symmetric as a bilinear mapping, it follows that g ⊗ is a symmetric tensor

annotations.note: Symmetry of the metric as a bilinear form is the `symm` field of `RiemannianMetric`, but Mathlib does not phrase it as symmetry of a section of `T*M ⊗ T*M`.

annotations.label: Metric in a vector bundle

anchor.snippet: More generally, one may speak of a metric in a vector bundle

annotations.note: `IsContinuousRiemannianBundle F E` is exactly a continuously varying inner product on the fibers of a real vector bundle.

annotations.label: Musical isomorphism (tangent–cotangent)

anchor.snippet: The metric tensor gives a natural isomorphism from the tangent bundle to the cotangent bundle , sometimes called the musical isomorphism

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Riesz musical isomorphism `toDual : E ≃ₗᵢ⋆[𝕜] StrongDual 𝕜 E` for a single inner product space, but no bundle-level `TM ≃ T*M` isomorphism.

annotations.label: S_g is a symmetric linear isomorphism

anchor.snippet: S g is a linear isomorphism

annotations.note: `InnerProductSpace.toDual` is a (conjugate-)linear isometric equivalence to the dual; the symmetric-isomorphism phrasing tied to a metric is not stated.

annotations.label: Correspondence between symmetric bilinear forms and symmetric isomorphisms

anchor.snippet: There is thus a natural one-to-one correspondence between symmetric bilinear forms

annotations.note: The bijection between symmetric bilinear forms on `V` and self-adjoint isomorphisms `V → V*` is not stated in this form.

annotations.label: Arclength of a parametric curve on a Riemannian manifold

anchor.snippet: The arclength of the curve is defined by

annotations.note: `pathELength I γ a b = ∫⁻ t in Icc a b, ‖mfderiv γ t 1‖ₑ` is exactly the arclength integral on a Riemannian manifold.

annotations.label: First fundamental form and line element

anchor.snippet: is called the first fundamental form associated to the metric, while ds is the line element

annotations.note: The metric inner product on tangent spaces stands in for the first fundamental form, but `lineElement`/`firstFundamentalForm` names are not introduced.

annotations.label: Coordinate-independence of length formulas

anchor.snippet: While these formulas use coordinate expressions, they are in fact independent of the coordinates chosen

annotations.note: `pathELength` is defined intrinsically via `mfderiv`, so coordinate-independence holds by construction (no separate change-of-coordinates lemma is needed).

annotations.label: Energy of a curve

anchor.section: The energy, variational principles and geodesics
anchor.snippet: another frequently defined quantity is the (kinetic) energy of the curve

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `kineticEnergy` of a curve in a Riemannian manifold.

annotations.label: Geodesic equations from variational principles

anchor.section: The energy, variational principles and geodesics
anchor.snippet: the geodesic equations may be obtained by applying variational principles to either the length or the energy

annotations.note: Geodesics and the Euler–Lagrange / geodesic equations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Canonical Borel measure from metric tensor

anchor.snippet: if M is a manifold with a (pseudo-)Riemannian metric tensor g , then there is a unique positive Borel measure

annotations.note: There is no canonical Riemannian volume measure on a manifold in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: if M is a manifold with a (pseudo-)Riemannian metric tensor g , then there is a unique positive Borel measure

annotations.label: Volume form on an oriented Riemannian manifold

anchor.snippet: it is possible to define a natural volume form from the metric tensor

annotations.note: `Orientation.volumeForm` defines a canonical top-form on an oriented finite-dimensional inner product space, but not on an oriented Riemannian manifold.

anchors.snippet: it is possible to define a natural volume form from the metric tensor

annotations.label: Two-dimensional Euclidean metric

anchor.snippet: the two-dimensional Euclidean metric tensor

annotations.note: `riemannianMetricVectorSpace F` puts the canonical Riemannian metric on any inner product space `F` (e.g. `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`), but no specifically two-dimensional Euclidean metric is named.

annotations.label: Euclidean metric in polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: Polar coordinates ( r , θ )

annotations.note: Polar coordinates exist (`Real.polarCoord`) but the Euclidean metric `dr² + r² dθ²` in those coordinates is not stated.

annotations.label: Euclidean metric in curvilinear coordinates

anchor.snippet: The metric tensor with respect to arbitrary (possibly curvilinear) coordinates

annotations.note: No `gᵢⱼ = ∂y·∂y` curvilinear-coordinate formula for the Euclidean metric in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Round metric on the sphere

anchor.section: The round metric on a sphere
anchor.snippet: The unit sphere in ℝ 3 comes equipped with a natural metric induced from the ambient Euclidean metric

annotations.note: No round Riemannian metric on `Metric.sphere` / `Sphere` is defined.

annotations.label: Minkowski metric (special relativity)

anchor.snippet: In flat Minkowski space ( special relativity ), with coordinates

annotations.note: The Minkowski metric is not in Mathlib (no Lorentzian metrics at all).

annotations.label: Schwarzschild metric

anchor.snippet: The Schwarzschild metric describes the spacetime around a spherically symmetric body

annotations.note: The Schwarzschild metric is not defined in Mathlib.
